title: JFTR's venues and voices for family theorizing and critical review
abstract: Journal of Family Theory & Review (JFTR) offers an array of creative and critical voices that champion theory advancement, methodological innovation, and scholarly contributions to social justice. In the June 2023 issue, we build on established JFTR venues for publishing theory and literature reviews, including Original Voices, Methodological Innovations, and Theoretical Advances. We also introduce two inaugural venues, Emerging Voices and Reflexive Voices, to expand theorizing about the interdisciplinary study of families and to continue to evolve as we explore new terrain beyond mainstream family science. In this overview, I define several venues for publishing articles in JFTR, in the hope of continuing to attract rigorous, cutting-edge scholarship that informs family theory and critical review. I offer my deepest gratitude to the authors and the many reviewers who crafted, critiqued, and revised these excellent contributions to the family science canon. JFTR's Original Voices feature invites highly prolific and esteemed scholars to augment their legacy of work. We are excited that Eduardo Bonilla-Silva, whose sociological work is synonymous with a structural perspective on racism and White habitus, has contributed an Original Voices paper to JFTR. At this time of increasing efforts to elevate family scholarship beyond additive understandings of “families + race,” Bonilla-Silva brings his racialized social systems approach to family science by challenging family scholars to examine themselves, in addition to their empirical work, as racialized subjects. He offers a comprehensive way to theorize structural racism by examining racialized concepts of society, ideology, and contestation, as well as the ways in which all of us—as human beings and scholars—are, in his words, “cogs in the racist machine.” His work invites family scholars to look deeply into the racism that is embedded in our own lived experience. Bonilla-Silva's theorization of the systems of domination that impact social and racial justice has been highly influential to critical race and intersectional scholars and continues to push the study of families forward by sharpening our ability to dismantle White supremacy and racial hierarchy in our lives and research. Emerging Voices papers amplify the concerns of the newest generation of family scholars—students and pre-tenure professionals—who are asked to bring brief and timely innovative theoretical thinking to the interdisciplinary study of families. I envision these papers as conceptualizing issues and frameworks that acutely resonate with the intersectional demographics of new scholars. The Emerging Voices feature of JFTR embodies the mission of encouraging, curating, and disseminating the sharp and creative ideas of students and early career professionals who have unique vantage points on the critical issues of our time. Emerging Voices papers are edited by JFTR's Deputy Editor Caroline Sanner, who invites inquiries and submissions in order to cultivate this genre of JFTR articles. In the current issue, Deadric Williams offers our inaugural Emerging Voices article. A leading voice of his generation, Williams challenges family scholars to become more sophisticated in their understanding of Black family disadvantage and White family advantage in order to improve the quantitative measurement of Black family experiences. Acknowledging that the critique of systemic racism in family science has been called out for decades, Williams offers a new theoretical model for studying the mechanisms of racial stratification in Black families that can move beyond the “epistemological negligence…that maintains racial inequality” and inspire new generations of scholars to eradicate inequality. Reflexive Voices unearths the messy side of scholarship, by revealing the person behind the science, and champions the often invisible and complicated intersections across lived experience and knowledge production. I know how challenging it is to write highly theorized reflexive narratives that are grounded in theory and in lived experience, yet, in my new role as JFTR Editor, I wanted to encourage family scholars to process the motivations that guide their work. To initiate this new venue, I enlisted four intersectional feminist scholars (Abbie Goldberg, Sarah Mitchell, TeKisha Rice, and Caroline Sanner) to develop their own version of how personal, professional, and political commitments have shaped their scholarship. I organized a “Working Paper Symposium” that was presented at the 2022 Theory Construction and Research Methodology Workshop at the National Council on Family Relations (NCFR) Annual Conference, with Sarah Allen serving as our wise discussant. What has resulted from this inaugural endeavor are four well-theorized, deeply moving, and often poetic, reflexive papers that will set the standard for what I hope are more critical reflexive analyses to come. I applaud our authors for their courage in exposing some of the underbelly of producing scholarship that matters, and for trusting themselves and our readers by taking these risks to reveal that which is often unnamed or erased. I envision students, instructors, researchers, and family members reading these papers in classrooms and in various discussion groups, delving deeply into how family relationships, trauma, and triumph influence what family scholars care enough about to theorize and investigate. In the first Reflexive Voices paper, Abbie Goldberg narrates the evolution of her scholarship on LGBTQ+ families in the context of her own experiences with marginalization and invisibility. She evokes the volatile sociopolitical and historical context in which LGBTQ+ families and LGBTQ+ family scholarship has unfolded. A pioneer in advancing understanding of diverse kinds of LGBTQ+ families, Goldberg's poignant depiction of challenges she faced within her family as the child of a lesbian mother provides a fresh perspective on research that has been primarily told from a parent's (not a child's) perspective. Particularly powerful is Goldberg's insightful analysis of how she now leverages reflexivity in her own positions of privilege by incorporating “collaboration, vulnerability, and accountability” in order to take her personally informed, impassioned scholarship to the public. In the second Reflexive Voices paper, Sarah Mitchell both deconstructs and integrates her racialized and gendered experiences of “always being cognizant of being a Black girl,” and figuring out how her sexual orientation fit into her evolving sense of herself as a Black bisexual woman. She traces with exquisite honesty her experiences of feeling “invisible, dismissed, and restricted” and connects the presence of these intersecting identities with the research she conducts on minoritized families. Mitchell describes how she navigates the tensions and challenges of working within primarily White academic institutions that are often at odds with the experiences of multiply marginalized individuals, like herself, with a passion for social justice. Her examples of finding joy and strength in mentoring her students and working beyond the university for social change are clear-eyed and inspiring. In the third Reflexive Voices paper, TeKisha Rice confronts the controversial, yet silenced, experience of facing her own minoritized identity as a Black person, and simultaneously, realizing her prejudice about nonheterosexuality that is rooted in her identity as a southern Christian woman. Rice utilizes her expertise on romantic relationships and as a Black scholar in primarily White institutions as the vehicle for discoveries about faith, race, and heterosexism. She names the complacency that comes from being a member of the majority (e.g., heterosexual), while at the same time, unveils the vulnerabilities that accompany minoritized race. Uniquely, she confronts head-on how unlearning her own heterosexist biases allowed her to examine systems of oppression more holistically. Rice invites others to travel with her across these dualities and experience more deeply Black family life from an intersectional perspective. In our final Reflexive Voices paper, Caroline Sanner breaks new ground in stepfamily research by tackling topics that are painful to study, including the family secrets we do not want others to know. Sanner reveals the fertile ground of structurally complex families with a depth that is only manifested if personally experienced. She introduces readers to family members she deeply loves, revealing their strengths, but also relying on their struggles to skillfully narrate a new story, often full of ambivalence, that deserves to be told. Sanner's “curiosity” about that which she has yet to ask her own family members, but can ask participants in her research, is a methodological insight that will empower new layers of meaning about how all family life is complicated, heartbreaking, and healing. Although JFTR is a theory and review journal, we also publish methodological innovations and analyses at the interface of theory and method. In this issue, Daniel Puhlman, Aya Shigeto, Gustavo Murillo-Borjas, Rakesh Maurya, and Virginia Vincenti offer an innovative methodology in which they integrate the clinical tool of genograms (a tree-like structure that traces family relationships and lineages), with qualitative data analysis and family systems theory to inform the entire research process. They developed a three-step analytical protocol, qualitative genogram analysis, to inform multiple relational and interactional patterns across families, beyond a simple comparison at the point of data collection or interpretation. This qualitative innovation offers the opportunity to generate empirical insights into complex family dynamics longitudinally. JFTR welcomes manuscripts that advance theory through methodological innovation. The process of theorizing and generating new interpretations of established frameworks is the life's blood of JFTR. In this issue, we offer four theoretical advances that provide new or revised conceptualizations of key family theories. In the first theoretical paper, Rhea Ashley Hoskin and Toni Serafini extend gendered and intersectional approaches in family science by introducing a framework oriented in critical femininities, namely femme theory. Advancing femme theory is a socially just practice, because of the persistent cultural relegation of femininity to a denigrated identity. Hoskin and Serafini highlight the operation of femmephobia as a regulatory system endemic to patriarchy, and address problems such as strict gender socialization, the valorization of masculinity, and violence in intimate relationships. Instead of these patriarchal institutions, femme theory offers a framework for theorists and practitioners to write new, emancipatory futures for all children and their families. Shivangi Gupta and Joyce Arditti, in the second paper, address Indian women's experience of secondary prisonization because of their incarcerated spouses, a hidden topic in family science. They expand intersectional feminist theorizing on multiply marginalized women to a global issue—the need for naming and conceptualizing the circumstances of low-income Indian women and their families of diverse castes, religions, and marital statuses. Their work sheds light on the isolation, hardship, and exploitation of women, who have the burden of extensive caregiving, yet are often blamed for the criminal activity of their spouses. Gupta and Arditti document how these women agentically resist their oppression and work for social change, offering a new context for interpreting women's lives through an intersectional feminist lens. In the third paper, Jenifer Bratter and Mary Campbell theorize about racial stratification, interracial couples, and multiracial families in both historical and contemporary contexts. They reveal the complexities of the color line, where most people in the United States still live and love in segregated ways, despite the increase in interracial families. Bratter and Campbell employ critical race theory and intersectionality to explain how entrenched racism and racial inequality remain today, even after many overtly racist laws and policies have been discarded. The structural barriers to equitable change are ubiquitous. Bratter and Campbell theorize that given these constraints, we need to “embrace a more multiracial vision of shared interests.” Our final theoretical paper, by Alyssa Clark, applies social exchange theory to conceptualize relational processes among asexual–allosexual couples, where one partner experiences little to no sexual attraction to others, and the other partner does experience sexual attraction. Clark examines sexuality-related exchanges dealing with sexual desire, behavior, coercion, and extradyadic experiences, to shed new light on the strengths and challenges in relationships that have limited understanding in the broader community. Clark's novel approach to theorizing about diverse relationship exchanges with one of the most established family theories pushes the boundaries of what has been understood as typical conventional behavior in intimate relationships, thus opening new doors to creative theorizing. Critical Book and Media Reviews comprise another key component of the JFTR repertoire. Looking ahead, we will publish several well-theorized book reviews on topics of great importance to family science. Our special issues are coming along beautifully as well. For example, in September 2023, JFTR will publish a special issue on Theorizing Singlehood, guest edited by Erin Lavender-Stott, Susan Brown, Karen Benjamin Guzzo, and Wendy Manning. They are bringing a truly interdisciplinary, international, and intersectional approach to new conceptualizations of singlehood. In addition to these many venues and voices in JFTR, I remind readers of our association with the Theory Construction and Research Methodology Workshop, both at the NCFR Annual Conference and as it continues online throughout the year. Theory Thursdays, facilitated by Kari Adamsons, Daniel Puhlman, and Jennifer Crosswhite, offer opportunities to interact with new and seasoned theorists on a particular framework. Past Theory Thursdays have covered attachment theory, bioecological theory, critical race theory, family systems theory, feminist theory, and symbolic interactionist theory. TCRM is a venue in which theory thrives. Often, many papers first presented at TCRM make their way to JFTR. We encourage authors to submit their high-quality theory and/or review papers to JFTR. We look forward to engaging with you on the exciting work of advancing theory, theorizing, and critical review.
